items.markdown:
The Electoral College is the official system used in the United States to elect the president and vice president every four years. It was established by the U.S. Constitution as a compromise between allowing citizens to vote directly for the president and allowing Congress to make the choice.[](https://www.archives.gov/electoral-college/about) [[1]](https://www.archives.gov/electoral-college/about)[[2]](https://bipartisanpolicy.org/explainer/the-electoral-college-simplified/)[[3]](https://history.house.gov/Institution/Electoral-College/Electoral-College/)[[4]](https://virginiahistory.org/sites/default/files/2024-09/7.4-Activity-3-Persuasive%20Writing.pdf)

The system operates through a group of representatives called electors. Every state is assigned a specific number of electors equal to its total representation in Congress: two for its senators, plus a variable number based on its representatives in the House. The more populous a state, the more electors it has.[](https://www.archives.gov/electoral-college/about) [[1]](https://www.archives.gov/electoral-college/about)[[2]](https://bipartisanpolicy.org/explainer/the-electoral-college-simplified/)[[3]](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college)

Here are a few key details about how it works:

- **The Electors:** There are 538 total electors in the Electoral College, which includes three representing Washington, D.C.[](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college) [[1]](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college)[[2]](https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/IF12682)
- **Winning the Election:** To win the presidency, a candidate must secure a majority of at least 270 electoral votes.[](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college) [[1]](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college)
- **Winner-Take-All:** In 48 states and Washington, D.C., the presidential candidate who wins the state's popular vote gets *all* of that state's electoral votes. Maine and Nebraska use a proportional system where electoral votes are divided based on congressional districts and the statewide vote.[](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college) [[1]](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college)
- **Popular vs. Electoral:** Because electors are awarded by state, it is possible for a candidate to win the nationwide popular vote but lose the presidency if their opponent secures more than 270 electoral votes.[](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college) [[1]](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college)

After citizens cast their ballots in November, the electors meet in mid-December to officially cast their votes for president and vice president. Congress then counts these votes in early January to finalize the election results.[](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college) [[1]](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college)[[2]](https://history.house.gov/Institution/Electoral-College/Electoral-College/)You can find more detailed information and the specific allocation of electoral votes for each state on the [USAGov](https://www.usa.gov/electoral-college) and [National Archives](https://www.archives.gov/electoral-college/about) websites.[[1]](https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/electoral-college)[[2]](https://www.cato.org/cato-journal/fall-2018/determining-state-preferences-electoral-college-1788-2016)[[3]](https://www.essexdems.org/post/electoral-college-what-it-is-and-how-it-shapes-elections)
